coredata.dc:title: In-situ coupling of N-doped carbon dots with manganese hexacyanoferrate as a cathode material for aqueous zinc-ion batteries
coredata.dc:creator: Xue, Yutao
Zhou, Hu
Ji, Zhenyuan
Shen, Xiaoping
Cao, Jiayi
Pu, Jinrui
Kong, Lirong
Yuan, Aihua
coredata.dc:description: Prussian blue analogs (PBAs) are a competitive class of aqueous Zn-ion batteries (AZIBs) cathode materials with an ideal three-dimensional open structure for rapid insertion and removal of Zn2+. Meanwhile, PBAs have the advantages of high working voltage and a simple synthesis process. However, these materials are inherently poor in electrical conductivity and are susceptible to structural collapse during cycling. These defects affect the performance of zinc storage in this type of material. Here, the integration of zero-dimensional nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) and in-situ grown manganese hexacyanoferrate (MnHCF) affords the MnHCF/NCDs cathode materials, which are applied to AZIBs. The introduction of NCDs modifies the active component of MnHCF effectively. Meanwhile, NCDs significantly enhance the overall electrical conductivity and structural stability of the composites and also provide abundant active sites for electrochemical reactions. Benefiting from these merits, the MnHCF/NCDs composite exhibits an excellent electrochemical zinc storage performance. It has a high discharge-specific capacity of 131.2 mAh g−1 at 50 mA g−1 and has outstanding cycling stability of 91% capacity retention after 1000 cycles at 1000 mA g−1. The electrochemical performance of PBAs-based cathodes for AZIBs may be improved using the straightforward and efficient method presented in this work.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Manganese hexacyanoferrate
Aqueous zinc ion batteries
Carbon dots
Synergistic effect
Cathode materials

The authors also acknowledge the support from the Instrumental Analysis Center, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the preparation of MnHCF/NCDs.
(a) SEM image of MnHCF; (b) SEM image, (c-d) TEM images and (e-f) HRTEM images of MnHCF/NCDs.
(a) XRD patterns of MnHCF/NCDs and MnHCF; (b) FT-IR spectra of MnHCF/NCDs and MnHCF; (c) Raman spectra of MnHCF/NCDs and NCDs; (d) TGA curves of MnHCF/NCDs and MnHCF.
High-resolution XPS spectra of (a) Mn 2p and (b) Fe 2p for MnHCF/NCDs and MnHCF.

CV curves of MnHCF/NCDs at 0.5 mV s−1 for the first three cycles; (b) GCD curves at 50 mA g−1 of the MnHCF/NCDs and MnHCF electrodes; (c) rate capability of the MnHCF/NCDs and MnHCF electrodes; (d) long-term cycling performance at 1000 mA g−1 of the MnHCF/NCDs and MnHCF electrodes.

(a) CV curves of the MnHCF/NCDs cathode at different scan rates; (b) The b values are calculated using the peak current-scan rate relationship; (c) Contributions to MnHCF/NCDs electrode's diffusion and capacitance control at different rates of scanning; (d) Nyquist plots of pure MnHCF and MnHCF/NCDs; (e and f) the galvanostatic intermittent titration technique curves and diffusion coefficients in a discharged state of pure MnHCF and MnHCF/NCDs.
(a) The GCD curves of the selected 1st and 2nd cycles; (b) Ex-situ XRD patterns of MnHCF/NCDs at different potentials; (c-h) SEM images of the MnHCF/NCDs electrode at varied potentials.
Schematic diagram of Zn2+ insertion/extraction in MnHCF/NCDs during charging and discharging.

head.ce:title: In-situ coupling of N-doped carbon dots with manganese hexacyanoferrate as a cathode material for aqueous zinc-ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental & Chemical Engineering, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang 212003, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
Jiangsu University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental & Chemical Engineering, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang 212003, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs)-modified manganese hexacyanoferrate (MnHCF) materials were used as cathode materials for aqueous zinc ion batteries using an in-situ co-precipitation method. This MnHCF/NCDs electrode material exhibited significantly enhanced zinc storage performance.

Prussian blue analogs (PBAs) are a competitive class of aqueous Zn-ion batteries (AZIBs) cathode materials with an ideal three-dimensional open structure for rapid insertion and removal of Zn2+. Meanwhile, PBAs have the advantages of high working voltage and a simple synthesis process. However, these materials are inherently poor in electrical conductivity and are susceptible to structural collapse during cycling. These defects affect the performance of zinc storage in this type of material. Here, the integration of zero-dimensional nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) and in-situ grown manganese hexacyanoferrate (MnHCF) affords the MnHCF/NCDs cathode materials, which are applied to AZIBs. The introduction of NCDs modifies the active component of MnHCF effectively. Meanwhile, NCDs significantly enhance the overall electrical conductivity and structural stability of the composites and also provide abundant active sites for electrochemical reactions. Benefiting from these merits, the MnHCF/NCDs composite exhibits an excellent electrochemical zinc storage performance. It has a high discharge-specific capacity of 131.2 mAh g−1 at 50 mA g−1 and has outstanding cycling stability of 91% capacity retention after 1000 cycles at 1000 mA g−1. The electrochemical performance of PBAs-based cathodes for AZIBs may be improved using the straightforward and efficient method presented in this work.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With continuous socioeconomic development, the global energy demand continues to grow. The vigorous development of sustainable energy storage technologies or devices is the key to alleviating the current environmental and energy problems [1,2]. Because of their high energy density, outstanding cycle performance, and low weight, rechargeable lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are now one of several energy storage technologies that are extensively employed in a variety of electronic items and electric vehicles [3]. However, the scarcity of lithium resources, and environmental or safety issues that cannot be ignored have limited the development of LIBs [4]. Aqueous metal-ion batteries, which include Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, and others, have recently gained significant attention as a wonderful option for extensive grid energy storage [5–7]. Among them, aqueous zinc ion batteries (AZIBs) have rapidly attracted extensive research interest owing to the higher theoretical specific capacity of metallic zinc, lower redox potential, and abundant reserves [8–10]. The current cathode materials for AZIBs are mainly manganese oxides [11] and vanadium oxides [12–14], but most of them have the problem of slow Zn2+ diffusion kinetics. Research on AZIBs is now concentrated on the creation of novel cathode materials with superior zinc storing capability.

Prussian blue analogs (PBAs), a typical three-dimensional (3D) framework material, can achieve a rapid insertion and deinsertion of various metal ions including Zn2+ [15,16]. The synergistic interaction of transition metal ions in PBAs can be used to maximize the zinc-storing capacity of the substance, including specific capacity and cyclic stability [17,18]. Manganese hexacyanoferrate (MnHCF) is a promising AZIBs cathode active material in PBAs. Both iron and manganese metal species in MnHCF can provide redox active sites for electrochemical reactions. However, the inherently poor conductivity of MnHCF limits its specific capacity output, and the framework structure of the material is prone to collapse during the Zn2+ ion intercalation, which may further lead to capacity decay [19,20]. To address these issues, academics have suggested a number of solutions, including doping the ions, modifying the morphology, and electrolyte optimization [21–24]. Lou's team developed a novel ion-exchange reaction to prepare Co-doped MnHCF hollow spheres (CoMn-PBA HSs) [25]. The hollow structure not only offers enough active sites and a sizable specific surface area, but it can also cushion the volume expansion of the substance during charging and discharging. In addition, the partial substitution of Co can stabilize the material structure and significantly improve cycling stability.

Due to their superb physical and chemical characteristics, carbon dots, a standard zero-dimensional carbon material, have lately been used in energy storage systems like supercapacitors and batteries [26–28]. Usually, carbon dots are composed of a core of hybridized carbon atoms and an amorphous surface containing functional groups of other elements. The diverse surface functional groups in carbon dots can provide more active binding sites when compounded with other materials, which in turn improves the overall electrochemical stability and charge transfer kinetics of the material [29,30]. In order to further improve the electrochemical performance of CDs, many attempts have been made on the structural design of CDs. Nitrogen doping is one of the most commonly used methods to improve the electrochemical performance of CDs. The atomic size of nitrogen is similar to that of carbon, but the electronegativity (3.04) is higher than that of carbon (2.55) [31]. Due to the electron-withdrawing ability of nitrogen, the adjacent carbon atoms become more positively charged, which promotes electron transport [32]. In addition, nitrogen doping usually leads to structural defects and produces more electrochemical active centers in CDs [33]. Xiong et al. synthesized the composites of Mn3O4 nanoparticles and nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) by in situ hydrothermal reaction and subsequent high-temperature calcination [27]. The NCDs-derived carbon skeleton offers a robust conducting network and a stable structure, and this composite material has a large number of active sites and excellent hydrophilicity. Based on these advantages, Mn3O4/NCDs electrodes exhibit an excellent zinc storage performance.

In this study, we prepared nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) modified manganese hexacyanoferrate (MnHCF), which may serve as the cathode material for AZIBs. It was found that the introduction of NCDs could improve the overall electrochemical kinetics and cycling stability of MnHCF, and thus the MnHCF/NCDs materials exhibit superior zinc storage properties.

The analytical-grade reagents utilized in this study were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd, and they were not previously purified. NCDs were obtained by hydrothermal reaction according to the previous literature method [34]. Specifically, citric acid (1.05 g) and ethylenediamine (350 µL) was dissolved in DI-water (10 mL). Then the solution was transferred to a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (30 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 16 h. After the reaction, the reactors were cooled to room temperature naturally. The products were dialyzed and freeze-dried to obtain NCDs.

In a typical synthesis, 1 mmol of K4Fe(CN)6 was dissolved in 25 mL of deionized water to create solution A. 2 mmol of MnSO4·H2O and 6 mmol of sodium citrate were dissolved in 25 mL of deionized water to create solution B. Thereafter, 10 mL of deionized water was concurrently added to both solutions, and the mixture was heated to 80 °C for one hour. Centrifugation was used to separate the MnHCF material, which was then cleaned with ethanol and deionized water. Lastly, the material was dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C.

20 mL of deionized water were combined with 10 mg of NCDs, which were then sonicated for 30 min to disperse. The aforementioned NCDs suspension was added, and it was stirred for 24 h, to a mixture of 25 mL of deionized water, 2 mmol of MnSO4·H2O, and 6 mmol of sodium citrate. The aforesaid mixture was then given 1 mmol of K4Fe(CN)6 and maintained at 80 °C for 1 h. The MnHCF/NCDs material was obtained by centrifugation and cleaned with ethanol and deionized water. The material was then dried at 60 °C in a vacuum oven.

SEM (Thermo Fisher Apreo S) and TEM (Transmission Electron Microscopy) were used for microstructural examination (TEM, FEI Tecnai F20). Shimadzu 6100Lab X-ray diffractometer was used to record the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns. Nicolet Nexus 470 infrared spectrometer was used to gather Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra. Using a Raman spectrometer (DXR, Thermo-Fisher) with 532 nm excitation, Raman data were captured. The NCDs percentage of MnHCF/NCDs was measured by thermogravimetric analysis (STA 449C). X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS) analysis was made with a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI device.

The cathode was created by combining the active material, acetylene black, and polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF), in a mass ratio of 7:2:1, with the solvent N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP). After the coating of the mixture on graphite paper, the electrode plate was dried for 12 h at 60 °C in a vacuum oven. The loading of active material is approximately 1.0 mg cm−2. The prepared electrode served as the working electrode in the construction of a CR2032 coin cell, whereas metal zinc foil served as the counter electrode. The electrolyte was ZnSO4 aqueous solution (2 M), and the separator was a commercial glass fiber (Waterman).

The materials were subjected to galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD) measurements using a battery test system (NEWARE-BTS-3008) with a voltage range of 0.5 to 2.0 V. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurement was conducted on an electrochemical workstation (CHI 760E, Shanghai Chenhua, China) with the same voltage window as the GCD test described above. Using the same electrochemical workstation, measurements for electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were also carried out with an amplitude of 5 mV and a frequency range of 105∼10−2 Hz.

MnHCF/NCDs composites were created using a typical in-situ co-precipitation method. The synthesis process was based on MnSO4·H2O, sodium citrate, NCDs, and K4Fe(CN)6 as raw ingredients. The schematic of the synthesis process is shown in Fig. 1 . Initially, MnSO4·H2O and nitrogen-doped carbon dots were adequately mixed in water to make Mn2+ ions adsorbed on NCDs. Then, K4Fe(CN)6 was introduced under stirring to generate the MnHCF/NCDs product, in which the sodium citrate as a surfactant helps to form regular MnHCF particles.

To analyze the morphological and structural characteristics of the products, SEM and TEM were used. The SEM image of MnHCF (Fig. 2
a) shows that the material is composed of agglomerated cubes with a size of ca. 0.5∼2 μm. After the addition of NCDs, the size of the particles did not changed significantly (Fig. 2 b-d). The composites' high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) characterization reveals the existence of NCDs (Fig. 2 e). In Fig. 2 f, where the lattice spacing of 0.35 nm corresponds to the (220) crystal planes of KMnFe(CN)6·2H2O, the lattice fringes of MnHCF are visible. The high-angle annular dark field (HAADF) scanning TEM (STEM) image (Fig. S1a) and elemental mapping (Fig. S1b-e) show that the four elements manganese, iron, carbon, and nitrogen are evenly distributed in the cube, which is consistent with the composition of MnHCF.

XRD characterization of the as-synthesized MnHCF/NCDs and MnHCF is shown in Fig. 3
a, the diffraction peaks of both materials correspond to the standard spectrum of KMnFe(CN)6·2H2O (JCPDS No. 51–1896) [25]. The principal diffraction peaks of these are, respectively, the (002), (220), and (040) crystal planes of KMnFe(CN)6·2H2O, which are situated at 17.5°, 24.9°, and 35.5°. The characteristic peaks of NCDs were not detected in the composites, which may be due to their weak crystallinity [35]. The XRD data demonstrate that the crystal structure of MnHCF is unaffected by the addition of NCDs. Fig. 3 b displays the samples' FT-IR spectra. O–H bonds are the source of the wide peaks at 3428 cm−1 and 1625 cm−1 [36], and the C≡N stretching vibration in MnHCF is responsible for the strong peak at 2067 cm−1 [36]. While the in-plane deformation of Fe–CN in the material is what causes the absorption peak at 599 cm−1 [37].

Fig. 3 c displays the sample's Raman spectrum. The characteristic peaks of C≡N were observed in MnHCF/NCDs. Fe–CN is responsible for the two sharp peaks at about 2076 cm−1 and 2115 cm−1 in particular [38]. Moreover, two broad peaks at around 1355 cm−1 and 1584 cm−1, which correspond to the D and G bands of carbon compounds, were seen [39]. MnHCF/NCDs displays higher ID /IG values (intensity ratio of D-band to G-band) than pure NCDs, which suggests that the NCDs in the composite could include more flaws. To measure the content of NCDs in the MnHCF/NCDs, TGA was performed on the material in an air atmosphere. As shown in Fig. 3 d, with increasing temperature, the weight decrease below 270 °C can be ascribed to the material losing its adsorbed and coordinated water. The continuous weight loss between 270 and 780 °C is due to the oxidation of NCDs and the decomposition of MnHCF [40]. After 780 °C, the weight of the sample gradually stabilized. Based on the TGA results, we estimated the content of NCDs in MnHCF/NCDs to be ca. 12.1 wt%.

XPS was used to analyze the material's chemical composition. The XPS spectrum (Fig. S2) shows that the as-synthesized MnHCF/NCDs and MnHCF contain C, N, O, Fe, and Mn elements. For the MnHCF/NCDs composite, two distinguishing peaks of Mn 2p3/2 and Mn 2p1/2 can be seen in the high-resolution Mn 2p XPS spectra (Fig. 4
a), which can be deconvoluted into Mn(II) (641.4/653.4 eV), Mn(III) (643.1/654.6 eV), and the satellite peak [36]. For the Fe 2p spectrum (Fig. 4 b), Fe(II) is represented by the two peaks at 708.7 and 721.6 eV, whereas Fe(III) is represented by the two peaks at 711.6 and 724.1 eV. Four peaks can be seen in the XPS spectra of C 1s (Fig. S3 a), including C–C, C≡N, C–N/C–O, and C=O [27]. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum (Fig. S3 b) can be deconvoluted into C≡N, C–N, and N–H [41]. The XPS spectra of Mn and Fe elements in MnHCF are also displayed in Fig. 4 a-b. Compared with that of MnHCF, Fe(III) in the Fe 2p3/2 characteristic peak of MnHCF/NCDs is shifted to higher binding energy. This indicates that the addition of NCDs has an obvious effect on the electron state of the Fe element in MnHCF, possibly due to the electron transfer between NCDs and Fe element.

A coin-type battery was assembled with the active material as the cathode and the metal zinc as the anode. And by using various electrochemical test methods, the obtained samples' zinc storage properties were analyzed. At a scan rate of 0.5 mV s−1, the materials' CV curves were recorded (Fig. 5
a and Fig. S4). As seen in Fig. 5 a, MnHCF/NCDs exhibits two pairs of redox peaks at 1.85/1.56 V and 1.94/1.73 V, this may be related to the redox reactions of Mn(III)/Mn(II) and Fe(III)/Fe(II) pairs, respectively [42,43]. From the second circle, almost overlapping redox peak locations and intensities can be seen in the CV curves. It showed that Zn2+ intercalation and extraction in the material are both very reversible. Due to the fact that MnHCF and MnHCF/NCDs have the identical redox pairs, their CV curves are similarly to each other (Fig. S4). Fig. 5 b compares the GCD curves of MnHCF/NCDs and MnHCF electrodes at a current density of 50 mA g−1. Compared to MnHCF, MnHCF/NCDs has a longer discharge plateau. It indicated that MnHCF/NCDs possesses superior zinc storage capacity. Specifically, the discharge-specific capacity of MnHCF/NCDs is 131.2 mAh g−1, which is much greater than that of MnHCF (109.3 mAh g−1). We conducted charge–discharge tests at various current densities in order to compare the rate performance of the materials. In Fig. 5 c, the reversible capacities ofMnHCF/NCDs materials are 117.2, 85.4, 62.5, 36.5, and 21.4 mAh g−1 at current densities of 50, 100, 200, 500, and 1000 mA g−1, respectively. As the current density dropped to 50 mA g−1, the discharge-specific capacity increased to 82.1 mAh g−1. In contrast, the MnHCF electrode exhibits lower specific capacity under the same conditions.

Fig. 5 d shows the cycle performance of MnHCF/NCDs and MnHCF at a high current density of 1000 mA g−1. The specific capacity of the MnHCF material declines rapidly in the first 200 cycles. And after 1000 cycles, its specific capacity is only 7.8 mAh g−1. This fast capacity decay of MnHCF may be due to the structural collapse and the dissolution of the material during cycling [44,45]. In contrast, the MnHCF/NCDs electrode exhibits a superior cycling stability. After 1000 cycles of charge–discharge test, the capacity retention rate is 91% (relative to the initial discharge specific capacity), and the specific capacity stays at 22.1 mAh g−1. This result indicates that the introduction of NCDs in MnHCF/NCDs can stabilize the electrode structure, and increasing the electrode's cycle life in the process. The energy density and power density computed based on the mass of the cathode active material are displayed in the Ragone diagram (Fig. S5). With a power density of 60.6 W kg−1, the MnHCF/NCDsⅡZn battery achieves an ultra-high energy density of 131.5 Wh kg−1. As the power density reaches 4358.1 W kg−1, the energy density remains at 20.6 Wh kg−1, which is higher than the battery of MnHCFⅡZn. In contrast to the reported PBAs-based AZIBs, the electrochemical performance of the MnHCF/NCDsⅡZn battery is also competitive (Table S1).

As demonstrated above, the ability of MnHCF to store zinc can be significantly increased by the introduction of NCDs. We also synthesized MnHCF/NCDs using 5 mg and 15 mg NCDs in order to further understand how NCDs affected the zinc storage performance of MnHCF/NCDs. The MnHCF/NCDs composite, which was created using 5 mg of NCDs, had poor rate performance and cycle stability, as shown in Fig. S6. This may be due to the weaker charge transfer ability caused by the insufficient amount of NCDs. When the amount of NCDs was increased to 15 mg, the obtained MnHCF/NCDs composites also exhibited decreased specific capacity, which may be due to the reduced relative content of MnHCF active component in the composite. Therefore, regulating the content of NCDs is also important for optimizing the zinc storage capacity of MnHCF/NCDs composites.

To probe the electrochemical kinetics of MnHCF/NCDs, we first calculated the redox pseudocapacitance contribution in the material. The MnHCF/NCDs CV curves were measured at various scan speeds ranging from 0.25 to 1.75 mV s−1 (Fig. 6
a). Eq. (1) may be used to represent the general connection between peak current (i) and scan rate (v):
(1)
i = a
v
b
where b represents diffusive or capacitive behavior, b close to 0.5 is controlled by ion diffusion, and close to 1 is controlled by pseudocapacitive behavior [46]. According to Fig. 6 b, the b values calculated from peaks 1–4 are 0.62, 0.69, 0.85, and 0.52 correspondingly, indicating that the kinetic behavior of the MnHCF/NCDs electrode is dominated by both the capacitive behavior and the diffusion behavior. Moreover, Eq.
(2) allows for the calculation of the capacitive behavior's contribution to capacity [47]:
(2)
i =
k 1
v +
k 2
v
1 / 2

According to Fig. 6 c, the capacitive contribution rises with increasing scan rate, going from 50.5% to 82.7%. The higher capacitance contribution rate also confirms the high rate capability of the MnHCF/NCDs composites.

In addition, the ion diffusion coefficients (D) of both electrodes were also evaluated by GITT [49]. The individual work-step test procedure for the GITT discharge phase is detailed in Fig. S8. Fig. 6 e illustrates the GITT curves for the MnHCF/NCDs and MnHCF discharging states. It can be seen that the discharge platform of MnHCF/NCDs is significantly longer than that of MnHCF, and MnHCF/NCDs shows longer discharge time. From the variation of ion diffusion coefficients (Fig. 6 f), it can be seen that the ion diffusion coefficients of MnHCF/NCDs are larger than those of MnHCF, further indicating the relatively faster migration of Zn2+ in MnHCF/NCDs.

Ex-situ XRD tests were used to analyze the structural changes in MnHCF/NCDs during various charge/discharge states. Fig. 7
a displays the chosen charge/discharge curves. In the ex-situ XRD pattern (Fig. 7 b), the electrode was first tested in its initial state and diffraction peaks were observed at 17.2, 24.6, 35.2, 38.9, 39.7, 43.1, and 44.0°, which matched the electrode's MnHCF active component. After the first discharging (corresponding to ‘a’ in Fig. 7 a), it can be observed that the diffraction peak at 17.2° is shifted to 16.8°. This results from the Zn2+ insertion into the MnHCF. A new diffraction peak is also found at 14.0°, which may be related to the generated ZnMnHCF. After the first full charge (corresponding to ‘b’ in Fig. 7 a), Zn2+ does not completely deintercalated from the material. When the second cycle is performed (corresponding to ‘b-h’ in Fig. 7 a), ZnMnHCF is involved in the subsequent reaction, which is consistent with the reaction mechanism previously reported in the literature [50]. The diffraction peaks at 16.8° and 14.0° were evident throughout the second cycle of charging and discharging, and their positions did not shift significantly during the whole process, confirming the good reversibility of the material. Moreover, the peaks of Zn4SO4(OH)6·nH2O (ZHS) by-product were observed throughout the cycle. ZHS can be generated from the electrochemical reaction between the electrolyte and the electrode surface [51]. In addition, SEM image and the corresponding EDS mapping (Fig. S9 and Table S3) also verified that Zn ions were successfully stored in MnHCF/NCDs materials, which was consistent with the ex-situ XRD results.

Meanwhile, we also investigated the morphological changes of MnHCF/NCDs electrodes during the charging and discharging process by SEM (Fig. 7 c-h). Firstly, on the surface of the electrode in the original state (Fig. 7 c), the cube shaped MnHCF can be seen (yellow arrow in the figure). At the beginning of the discharging process (Fig. 7 d), the morphology does not change significantly compared to the initial state. As the discharging process gradually proceeds (Fig. 7 e), ZHS by-products (red arrows in the figure) start to appear on the electrode surface. When discharged to 0.5 V (Fig. 7 f), the electrode surface is almost completely covered by ZHS by-products. While charging, the ZHS gradually decreases (Fig. 7 g). Until the 2.0 V full charging (Fig. 7 h), the ZHS almost completely disappears and the MnHCF cubes can be observed again. From the SEM images at different potential states, it can be inferred that the electrode material has a good reversibility throughout the charging and discharging cycles.

We show a schematic picture of the charging and discharging process of MnHCF/NCDs based on the aforementioned investigations. As shown in Fig. 8 , during the first charging and discharging, Zn replaced part of the Mn connected to N. Afterwards, in the subsequent electrochemical reaction, more and more Zn ions were inserted into the interior of the material and occupied the Mn sites and gaps, forming the more stable ZnyMnxFe(CN)6.

In conclusion, we have synthesized a nitrogen-doped carbon-dot modified manganese-based Prussian blue analog by in situ co-precipitation and applied it to aqueous zinc ion battery cathode materials. In the manganese-based Prussian blue analog, the dual redox reaction provided by two transition metals (manganese and iron) increases the specific capacity of the material. The introduction of carbon dots raises the composite's electrical conductivity and buffers the volume change of the material during cycling. The resulting MnHCF/NCDs composites exhibit excellent zinc storage performance. It has a high specific capacity of 131.2 mAh g−1 at 50 mA g−1 and outstanding cycling stability of 91% capacity retention after 1000 cycles at 1000 mA g−1. This study shows that Prussian blue analogs' zinc storage ability may be considerably enhanced by carbon dots, which may drive the application of carbon dots in PBAs-based AZIBs cathode materials.

Yutao Xue: Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Conceptualization, Investigation, Data curation. Hu Zhou: Writing – review & editing, Resources. Zhenyuan Ji: Methodology, Resources. Xiaoping Shen: Methodology, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Jiayi Cao: Validation, Investigation. Jinrui Pu: Validation, Investigation. Lirong Kong: Methodology, Resources. Aihua Yuan: Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful for financial supports from National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22279047), and the project of the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (China). The authors also acknowledge the supportfromtheInstrumental Analysis Center, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2023.157580.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
